name: My Account
classification_notes: No typeface information was available on this page; it is a login/account page. Category defaults have been applied as placeholders.
summary: No typeface data was found on this page, which is a login screen rather than a typeface specimen.
description: The provided URL resolves to a customer account login page for Tipotype, containing no typeface specimen, description or product information. No meaningful typeface entry can be constructed from this content.
foundry_blurb: No foundry copy about a typeface was present on this page.

name: Corporate License
classification_notes: This page does not describe a specific typeface; it is a licensing enquiry form for Tipotype's corporate clients. No typeface details are available to classify.
summary: A bespoke corporate licensing scheme from Tipotype, covering desktop, web, app and broadcast use without quantity restrictions.
description: Tipotype's corporate licence is a tailored agreement for businesses that need unrestricted typography usage across multiple channels. It covers desktop installation, web traffic, app deployments and broadcast audiences, with terms adapted to the client's legal and technical environment. Enquiries are submitted via a contact form, after which Tipotype provides a custom quote.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe themselves as an independent type foundry specialising in corporate projects. Their corporate licence is designed to simplify the legal and administrative management of a company's typography, removing usage limits and quantity restrictions across all media and adapting to each client's specific legal, technological and communication needs.

name: Case Studies
classification_notes: This page is a case studies listing, not a specific typeface page. No single typeface can be classified from this content.
summary: A collection of case studies from Tipotype showing how their typefaces have been used in brand identity and editorial projects worldwide.
description: This page lists Tipotype's case studies, covering typefaces such as Gazzetta, Fieldwork, Rufina, Mundial and Brother 1816 deployed across clients including Major League Baseball, NASA, Barclays France and the AA. The projects span editorial, transport, banking and cultural sectors. No single typeface is the subject of this page.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype present a series of case studies showing how their type designs have been adopted by brands and institutions across the globe, from custom commissions for Major League Baseball to Rufina for the British monarchy.

name: Contact Us
classification_notes: No typeface information is present on this page; it is a contact form page. Category fields are placeholder values only.
summary: This URL points to Tipotype's contact form page, not a typeface product page.
description: No typeface information is available at this URL. The page contains only a contact form with fields for name, email, company, city/country, subject and message. No font name, designer, weights or other typographic details are present.
foundry_blurb: This page is Tipotype's contact form and contains no typeface-specific copy or product information.

name: Signat
classification_notes: Tipotype describe Signat explicitly as a refined grotesque, built on precise optical criteria with structural consistency rather than eccentricity, placing it firmly within the grotesque sans-serif tradition.
summary: A structurally disciplined grotesque from Tipotype, calibrated for optical consistency across institutional identity, editorial and brand systems.
description: Built on precise optical criteria rather than expressive eccentricity, Signat aims for authority through balance and visual continuity. Its strategic neutrality lets it move between technology environments, fashion contexts and high-end editorial without losing character. Eighteen styles are available, with italics and a range from Extra Light to Extra Bold.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Signat as emerging from a rigorous structural logic guided by optical precision, where curves, junctions and proportions are calibrated for visual continuity. They position it as a grotesque that conveys authority without harshness and presence without excess, suited to institutional identities, technology, fashion, and brand systems that require continuity and control.

name: Capitale
classification_notes: The foundry describes Capitale as exploring the transition from serif to sans, referencing early English typefaces moving away from classical serifs with short serifs and Roman proportions — placing it firmly in the transitional category with geometric underpinning.
summary: A transitional serif from Tipotype that traces the historical move from classical Roman letterforms toward more functional, rational type design.
description: Rooted in the tradition of Roman capitals carved in stone, Capitale carries structural balance and formal restraint across 18 styles spanning Light to Black with matching italics. Subtle calligraphic inflections sit within a geometric framework, giving the family a composed tension between historical reference and contemporary utility. Suited to editorial, publishing and identity work where quiet authority matters more than expressiveness.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Capitale as a contemporary typeface exploring the transition from serif to sans, born as a remembrance of when modernity was emerging from classicism. It belongs to the tradition of Roman capital letters carved in stone, preserving structural balance and formal restraint, while also capturing the spirit of early industrial typefaces that adopted a more functional and austere attitude. Built on a sober geometric structure with subtle calligraphic inflections, it is designed to meet present demands while remaining conscious of its heritage.

name: Rubika
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a neo-grotesque sans, with organic shapes and expressive personality typical of the genre, distinguished by sharp contrasts and a friendly character.
summary: A neo-grotesque sans designed by Lucas Pozzobon that pairs organic letterforms with sharp contrast for a warm, expressive character.
description: Designed by Lucas Pozzobon for Tipotype, Rubika is a neo-grotesque that introduces personality through organic shapes and sharp stroke contrast without compromising legibility. The family spans 16 styles with weights from Light to Black, each accompanied by italics, giving it enough range for editorial, packaging and entertainment contexts. Its tone sits between playful and professional, making it a practical choice for brand work that needs some warmth.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Rubika as a neo-grotesque sans with a distinctive personality, blending organic shapes, sharp contrasts and a friendly, expressive attitude. They present it as a typeface that conveys warmth and dynamism while remaining legible and structurally solid, suited to packaging, entertainment, editorial and high-end brand environments.

name: Humant
classification_notes: Explicitly Bauhaus-inspired with modular construction based on circles, squares and triangles; clean minimalist letterforms without decoration place it firmly in geometric sans territory.
summary: A Bauhaus-inspired geometric sans from Tipotype with modular letterforms built on circular and rectilinear construction, available in 18 styles.
description: Built on the formal logic of the Bauhaus movement, Humant uses circles, squares and triangles as the basis for clean, symmetrical letterforms. Eighteen styles span a range from Thin to Extra Black, with at least one italic included, giving the family enough range for editorial, poster and branding work. The modular structure keeps the design consistent across weights, avoiding the looseness that often afflicts geometric revival faces.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Humant as a geometric typeface inspired by the Bauhaus design movement, characterised by clean minimalist lines and circular shapes that emphasise balance and symmetry. They note its modular structure and modern yet timeless aesthetic, positioning it for branding, posters and editorial design, and cite the Bauhaus principle of merging art and technology as the driving concept.

name: Suncoast
classification_notes: The family comprises two distinct subfamilies: Suncoast Grotesque, a functional grotesque sans, and Suncoast Humanist, a warmer humanist variant. The overall category is sans-serif, with the grotesque subfamily being the primary named style.
summary: A dual-subfamily sans in 32 styles, pairing a functional grotesque with a warmer humanist variant for commercial and packaging work.
description: Suncoast splits into two complementary subfamilies, Grotesque and Humanist, intended to be mixed within the same project to modulate typographic tone. The design draws on mid-20th-century lettering traditions while keeping a clean, contemporary finish suited to gourmet packaging, cosmetics, wine labels and retail campaigns. Both subfamilies share italic cuts across a range of weights from Thin to Black.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Suncoast as a contemporary typeface with a retro soul, inspired by the lettering that defined 20th-century visual identity. It blends the functional solidity of grotesques with the flowing warmth of humanist styles, and is specially designed for commercial products including gourmet packaging, retail campaigns, wine labels and cosmetic lines. The two subfamilies, Grotesque and Humanist, are designed to be mixed and matched to suit a brand's context and personality.

name: Formiga
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist and expressive display typeface aimed at impactful headlines and packaging, placing it firmly in the display category with humanist proportions informing its letterform construction.
summary: A humanist display family in seven weights by Lucas Pozzobon, selected for the 9th Bienal de Tipos Latinos, suited to bold headlines and packaging.
description: Drawn by Lucas Pozzobon and released by Tipotype, Formiga runs from Light to Black across seven weights with humanist proportions that give it warmth at large sizes. The family supports 38 languages and includes OpenType features to aid legibility in specific character combinations. Its fresh, high-contrast feel suits editorial headlines, posters and packaging where a bold contemporary voice is needed.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Formiga as a humanist and expressive display typeface, ideal for impactful headlines in modern and contemporary design contexts and well-suited to packaging with a fresh, bold and powerful style. The family offers seven weights from Light to Black, supports 38 languages, and includes OpenType features to improve legibility of specific characters. It was selected for the 9th Bienal de Tipos Latinos.

name: Morph
classification_notes: Morph is a display type system with multiple decorative variants including stencil, inline, outline, doubleline, and solid cuts, placing it firmly in the display/decorative category with no conventional text-setting intent.
summary: A 14-style display type system from Tipotype offering stencil, inline, outline, doubleline and solid variants for high-impact typographic work.
description: Built around a clean, modern skeleton, Morph branches into six structural variants including stencil in two weights, inline, doubleline, outline and solid light, giving designers a consistent visual language across contrasting effects. Each variant ships with over 500 glyphs, multiple numeral styles, an extensive arrow set and stylistic alternates. The family is aimed squarely at posters, packaging, titles and brand identity work where graphic distinction matters.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Morph as a display type system built for typographic identity and impact across posters, titles and packaging. They note its versatile, modern and clean character, and highlight that each variant includes over 500 glyphs, multiple numeral styles, an extensive arrow set and stylistic alternates, making it a broad type system.

name: Mundial Narrow
classification_notes: Mundial Narrow is a condensed sans-serif that blends multiple typographic traditions with a humanist sensibility, referencing both modern and historical influences through its letterform construction.
summary: A narrow companion to the Mundial family, offering proportions suited to both corporate identity and editorial text work across 20 styles.
description: Derived from the wider Mundial typeface, Mundial Narrow carries the same cosmopolitan sensibility into tighter proportions suitable for identity systems and editorial layouts alike. Twenty styles span a range of weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, blending typographic traditions to balance modernity with historical confidence. Its condensed form makes it practical for tight column widths without sacrificing readability at text sizes.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Mundial Narrow as inheriting the shapes and cosmopolitan spirit of its parent typeface, Mundial, while offering proportions that work equally well in corporate identity and text settings. They note that the design combines different typographic traditions into a synthesis of modernity and historical confidence, with an expanded weight range that extends its use into editorial projects.

name: Typocaster
classification_notes: A sans-serif with expressive, attention-grabbing shapes balanced against readability, spanning five widths and seven weights from Thin to Black in the grotesque tradition.
summary: A wide-ranging sans-serif family by André Párraga, built around the visual energy of rock music and live performance.
description: Spanning five widths and seven weights, Typocaster runs from Thin to Black with narrow and wide variants totalling 35 styles. The letterforms balance expressive, attention-grabbing shapes with solid readability, making the family usable across display headlines and text settings. OpenType features and support for over 200 languages broaden its reach beyond its rock-and-festival origins.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Typocaster as a versatile sans serif family built with the energy of rock music, concerts, and festivals at its core. Designed by André Párraga, it balances expressive shapes with solid readability and spans five widths, seven weights, and OpenType features. With support for over 200 languages, it is positioned for bold display work and reliable text settings alike.

name: Gravita
classification_notes: Gravita blends geometric precision drawn from modernist imprint typefaces with humanist sensitivity, offering two distinct subfamilies (GEO and HUM) that sit between classic geometric and humanist sans-serif traditions.
summary: A 36-style sans-serif family split into geometric and humanist subfamilies, each spanning nine weights with italics, aimed at corporate and editorial applications.
description: Gravita offers two predefined subfamilies, GEO and HUM, each with nine weights from Extra Light to Heavy and corresponding italics, giving 36 styles in total. The design draws on the geometric precision of modernist imprint typefaces, tempered by a contemporary humanist sensibility in its proportions and curves. It is positioned for corporate communication where clarity and a degree of authority are required.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Gravita as a typeface family that conveys security and respect through classic proportions and modern precision drawing. It is designed for corporate communication where confidence and transparency need to be perceived, and takes inspiration from geometric modernist imprint typefaces reinterpreted through a 21st-century humanist lens.

name: Thermal
classification_notes: Thermal is a serif with roots in 16th-century French typography, particularly Granjon, blending classical transitional serif construction with contemporary weight extremes drawn from woodtype and 1970s lettering traditions.
summary: A variable serif by Fernando Díaz that spans Granjon-influenced text weights to woodtype-inflected display extremes across two axes: weight and optical size.
description: Designed by Fernando Díaz and awarded a TDC Certificate for Typographic Excellence, Thermal is built as a variable font with weight and optical size axes, yielding 48 styles suited to both body text and large-scale setting. The text weights draw on the 16th-century French typefaces of Robert Granjon for warmth and reading rhythm, while the extreme thin and black cuts borrow from 19th-century woodtype, 1970s sharp lettering and the bold work of Oscar Ogg. A distinctive feature is the italic's 20-degree inclination, rooted in the Ascendonica Cursive of 1571, which creates a pronounced contrast against the upright.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Thermal as an exploration of balance and contrast, combining classical typographic elegance with the sharpness of contemporary design. Conceived as a variable font with weight and optical size axes, its text weights offer a relaxed interpretation of Robert Granjon's 16th-century work, while the extreme weights draw on 19th-century woodtype, 1970s lettering and the bold work of Oscar Ogg. The italic's 20-degree inclination references the Ascendonica Cursive of 1571, creating a dichotomous identity that spans historical reference and contemporary application.

name: Mozaic
classification_notes: The family includes a Hum (humanist) subfamiy and a Geo (geometric) variant, with italics, indicating a dual-axis humanist sans-serif family. The naming conventions and specimen styles confirm a humanist classification as the primary category.
summary: A 30-style sans-serif family from Tipotype that pairs a humanist branch with a geometric variant and italics, aimed at wayfinding and broad editorial use.
description: Mozaic spans 30 styles across humanist and geometric sub-families, with weights running from Thin to Black and a dedicated italic range. The specimen copy emphasises wayfinding systems and signage applications, and the family carries broad Latin script coverage suited to multilingual projects. The two sub-families — Hum and Geo — give designers the option to shift between warmer, calligraphically informed forms and more rational geometric shapes within a single family.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Mozaic as a family where individual beauty contributes to a collective identity, enriched by exchange and perfected by diversity. They position it as a sum of parts that gains strength through variety, and highlight its thorough coverage of Latin alphabet-based language families.

name: Gazzetta
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a condensed display family with neo-grotesque nature, featuring softened curves, sharp joins and a slight reverse contrast in heavier weights — characteristics that push it firmly into the display category despite grotesque origins.
summary: A condensed neo-grotesque display family by Edwin Moreira, built across eight weights with matching slanted styles and a distinctly editorial character.
description: Designed with newspapers, magazines and book covers in mind, the family takes its name from the 16th-century Venetian gazette and leans into that heritage with a confident, condensed construction. Softened curves and sharp stroke joins give the heavier weights an expressive, high-contrast quality while remaining legible at smaller editorial sizes. Sixteen styles span Thin to Black with slanted counterparts throughout, supported by OpenType features including oldstyle figures, stylistic alternates and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Gazzetta as a condensed font family with a display character and neo-grotesque nature, friendly and energetic, with softened features, very sharp joins and a slight reverse contrast in its thicker weights. The family comprises eight weight variables and their slanted versions, and is suited to book covers, newspapers, magazines, posters, large-format materials, websites and apps. It received a Bronze Ladawards in 2022.

name: Axios Pro
designer: Rodrigo López Fuentes & Sergio Leiva Whittle
classification_notes: Axios Pro blends the rational geometry of early 20th-century geometric sans-serifs with humanist proportions drawn from renaissance type traditions, placing it at the intersection of geometric and humanist sans-serif.
summary: A geometric sans with humanist underpinnings, offering 10 weights with italics and variable font support across more than 200 Latin languages.
description: Designed by Rodrigo López Fuentes and Sergio Leiva Whittle, Axios Pro grafts early 20th-century geometric rationalism onto uppercase proportions drawn from the roman canon and a lowercase built on humanist ductus. The result reads as clean and contemporary while retaining a classical warmth in text. Twenty styles, a variable font file and over 1,000 glyphs per style make it broadly applicable across branding, editorial, headline and signage work.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Axios Pro as combining the rational language of early 20th-century geometric sans-serifs with an underlying structure rooted in renaissance type; uppercase proportions follow the roman canon while the lowercase observes humanist ductus. Available in 20 styles across 10 weights plus variable font format, with over 1,000 glyphs per style, support for more than 200 Latin languages and an extensive suite of OpenType features including small caps, swashes, ligatures and multiple figure styles.

name: Fisterra
classification_notes: Fisterra is explicitly described as an informal, serif, display typeface in two stylistic flavours sharing a condensed uppercase skeleton, placing it firmly in the display category with a serif subclassification.
summary: A two-style display serif family built on a shared condensed uppercase skeleton, offering one soft humanist variant and one sharp angular variant.
description: Fisterra splits into two contrasting styles: Morte, characterised by voluptuous curves and a humanist softness, and Fora, defined by precise sharp angles. Both share a single condensed uppercase skeleton, with alternate characters and ligatures expanding the design space between them. The family covers 143 languages across Latin-based scripts and ships in OTF, TTF, and several web formats.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Fisterra as one typeface seen from two perspectives, reflecting the duality between calm and intensity. Morte brings softness and humanism through voluptuous curves, while Fora delivers precision through sharp angles. Both styles share a condensed uppercase skeleton, with expanded nuances in alternate characters and ligatures.

name: Mundial
classification_notes: Mundial synthesises characteristics from multiple typographic traditions into a cohesive sans-serif. Its mix of gestures from different eras suggests a humanist approach rather than strict geometric or grotesque classification.
summary: A 14-style sans-serif from TipoType that draws on gestures from multiple typographic traditions to form a single cohesive style with broad language coverage across 219 languages.
description: Spanning six weights from Hair to Black with matching italics, Mundial is designed around the idea of condensing characteristics from different typographic eras and cultures into one unified voice. The family supports 219 Latin-based languages and ships with a wide OpenType feature set including stylistic alternates, old-style numerals and self-building fractions. Its name, translating as 'Worldwide', signals an ambition for broad application across editorial, identity and international publishing contexts.
foundry_blurb: TipoType describe Mundial as a typeface whose name is a statement: the idea of synthesising characteristics from different typographic traditions into a single style. Gestures from various eras and cultures are visible but always subordinate to the overall cohesion, with the sum exceeding its individual parts. They position it as a typeface for a time when individual identity marks are the best means of building something collective.

name: Rustica
classification_notes: Tipotype describe Rustica as a 'GeoHumanist' sans serif, blending humanist architecture with the geometric precision of early 20th-century grotesques, placing it clearly in the humanist sans tradition with geometric influence.
summary: A GeoHumanist sans serif from Tipotype blending humanist letter architecture with the geometric discipline of early 20th-century grotesques across 18 styles.
description: Drawn by the TipoType Team, Rustica sits between humanist warmth and geometric rigour, drawing on the foundry's earlier Rotunda as a structural starting point. Eighteen styles span seven weights from Thin to Black, each with matching italics, and the family covers 219 languages across a 729-glyph character set with a broad OpenType feature set including old-style numerals, stylistic alternates and self-building fractions. It suits text-heavy editorial work and identity projects where a clean, measured sans is needed without the coldness of a strict geometric.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Rustica as a return to humanist typographic values updated for 21st-century communications, combining the closeness and proximity of humanist design with the certainty and precision of geometric forms. Born from the DNA of their awarded font Rotunda, it is positioned as a GeoHumanist sans serif that looks to the past to move confidently into the future.

name: Rotunda
classification_notes: Rotunda blends geometric construction with humanist warmth and grotesque neutrality, placing it squarely in the humanist grotesque subcategory with British undertones that give it a sober, classic character.
summary: A 16-style sans-serif from Tipotype that mixes geometric structure with humanist and grotesque traits, winner of a Communication Arts 2021 Typography Annual award.
description: Rotunda draws from geometric, humanist and grotesque traditions without reading as a compromise, giving it a measured personality with British undertones. The family runs across five weights from Hairline to Black with matching italics, totalling 16 styles, and covers 219 languages across a 634-glyph character set. OpenType features include old-style numerals, stylistic alternates and self-building fractions, making it equally suited to editorial and body text work.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Rotunda as blending the best of three worlds — geometric, humanist and grotesque — with a strong personality and British undertones that make it a stylish and sober classic. They position it as a versatile all-terrain font that is strong and elegant, modern and classic, with thorough language coverage across Latin-based scripts.

name: Brother 1816
classification_notes: Brother 1816 explicitly blends geometric construction with humanist stroke qualities, offering alternate characters that let users shift between the two approaches. This hybrid positioning places it between pure geometric and humanist sans traditions.
summary: A 32-style sans-serif family that blends geometric construction with humanist strokes, offering over 20 alternates to shift between the two sensibilities.
description: Designed by Fernando Díaz and Ignacio Corbo, Brother 1816 sits between geometric and humanist sans traditions, with alternate characters allowing designers to move along that spectrum within a single project. The 32 fonts split into two groups, normal and printed, each covering 16 weights with 547 characters per style including swashes, fractions and ordinals. Hinting instructions make it reliable on screen at small sizes, while its more assertive geometric qualities suit headlines and posters.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Brother 1816 as a flexible, solid typeface that mixes geometric shapes with humanistic strokes simultaneously. With over 20 alternates per weight, designers can choose a pure geometric feel, a humanistic one or something in between, making it suitable for branding, editorial and signage at any scale. It was a MyFonts bestseller in 2016 and featured in Typographica's favourite typefaces of that year.

name: Fieldwork
classification_notes: Fieldwork offers two subfamilies: a geometric variant (Geo) with clean, structured strokes and a humanist variant (Hum) with double-storey a and g, placing it broadly within the geometric/humanist sans spectrum.
summary: A 24-style sans-serif family from TipoType split into geometric and humanist subfamilies, each weight hand-drawn rather than interpolated.
description: Each of the 24 styles was drawn individually using optical evaluation, with curves and details adjusted per weight rather than generated through automated interpolation. The Geo subfamily offers clean geometric strokes suited to display and wayfinding work, while the Hum subfamily introduces double-storey letterforms for a warmer, more readable texture. The family is supported by swashes, alternates and old-style numerals, and covers 227 Latin-based languages across 904 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: TipoType describe Fieldwork as a revival of manual typography production traditions, with each of its 24 variants drawn by hand based on optical evaluation rather than lab interpolation. Curves and details were specifically adjusted per weight to suit distinct stroke requirements. The family combines geometric strokes in the Geo variants with humanistic warmth in the Hum variants, and includes swashes, alternates and italics for every weight. It won a Clap Platinum award in 2020 for Best Typeface Font for Titles.

name: Rufina
classification_notes: Rufina blends high-contrast Bodoni-influenced forms with calligraphic touches, placing it firmly in the didone tradition of modulated, vertical-stress serifs.
summary: A Bodoni-influenced display serif from Tipotype with calligraphic warmth, ornamental alternates and 147-language Latin coverage across nine styles.
description: Drawing on the high contrast and vertical stress of Bodoni, Rufina softens its rigour with a calligraphic undercurrent, making it feel refined rather than cold. The family spans two weights with italic counterparts, plus two sets of alternate cuts, each carrying a selection of illustrative ornaments for decorative use. It won a Tipos Latinos award in 2012 and suits editorial, packaging and branding work where elegance and period character are required.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Rufina as characterised by simplicity, delicacy and elegance, conceived from dark-text-on-light-background combinations. They call it refined and distant, seducing the viewer subtly through a blending of contrasty Bodoni-influenced forms with the emotive touch of a calligrapher's pen. The family comprises two weights, their italic counterparts and two sets of alternate cuts, each including a selection of illustrative ornaments.

name: Aila
classification_notes: Aila is explicitly described as a slab serif built on a Roman structure with organic features; the tension between rigid slab construction and rhythmic, cursive italic forms places it firmly in the slab category with an organic or humanist inflection.
summary: A slab serif by Francis Requena that plays structural Roman forms against organic details, with particularly expressive italic cuts suited to body text and editorial work.
description: Built on a Roman structure but softened by organic features, Aila creates a deliberate tension between rigid slab construction and flowing rhythm. That tension is most visible in the heavier weights, Bold and Black, and finds its fullest expression in the italics, which reinterpret classic cursive ductus to produce swift, rhythmic counterparts to the upright styles. Each of the eight styles carries an extended character set covering 198 languages, making it a practical choice for multilingual editorial projects.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Aila as a surprising slab serif grounded in realistic Roman structure but distinguished by unique organic features that create an expressive tension between structure and rhythm. The heavier weights and italic forms are highlighted as the clearest expression of this dynamic, with the italics offering a re-interpretation of classic cursive ductus for rhythmic, editorial use. Each style ships with an extended character set designed specifically for editorial design.

name: Libertad
classification_notes: Libertad blends humanist and grotesque models into a sans-serif family, a hybrid approach that sits between the warmth of humanist sans and the structure of a grotesk. The designer explicitly describes it as mixing both traditions.
summary: A humanist-grotesque hybrid by Fernando Díaz, offering seven interpolation-theory-weighted styles with dynamic italics and broad language support across 211 languages.
description: Designed by Fernando Díaz and awarded a Platinum CLAP Award at Tipos Latinos 2016, Libertad pairs balanced upright weights with more expressive italics across seven steps from Thin to Extra Bold. The weight distribution follows Luc(as) de Groot's Interpolation Theory, keeping optical consistency across headline and text sizes. With over 800 glyphs per weight and coverage for more than 70 languages, it is a practical workhorse for multilingual editorial and publishing work.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Libertad as a sans-serif that mixes humanist and grotesk models, with its standout feature being the contrast between balanced regulars and dynamic italics. The family uses Luc(as) de Groot's Interpolation Theory to calculate seven weights plus matching italics, making it suitable for both headlines and small text. It contains over 800 characters per weight, supporting more than 70 languages, and received a Platinum CLAP Award at Tipos Latinos 2016.

name: Arya
classification_notes: Arya is explicitly described as a display typeface built on Roman proportions, with multiple stroke-count variants designed to be layered, placing it firmly in the display category with a structured, geometric approach.
summary: A layerable display typeface built on Roman proportions, offered in solid, double-stroke and triple-stroke variants that can be stacked for typographic effect.
description: Three structural variants, Single, Double and Triple, vary the number of drawn lines to create distinct visual weights while remaining combinable as layers. The Triple family extends further with Alt, Slant and Capsule cuts, the last designed for quick label work in both white and black versions. Glyph count sits at 227 characters across 143 Latin-based languages, with OpenType kerning included.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Arya as a display typeface based on Roman proportions. Single is solid, Double is sturdy but light, and Triple is versatile with alternates; all three can be combined in layers. The Capsule versions in white and black are intended for quick, simple and elegant labels.

name: Brother XS & XL
classification_notes: Mixes geometric shapes with humanistic strokes, offering both condensed and expanded widths; the designers explicitly describe it as blending geometric and humanistic qualities, placing it between classic geometric and humanist sans categories.
summary: A condensed and expanded extension of Brother 1816, offering 32 styles across two width extremes for use from tight text columns to expressive display settings.
description: Brother XS & XL adds narrow and wide variants to the Brother 1816 type system, designed by Fernando Díaz and Ignacio Corbo. The XS family provides 16 condensed weights suited to tight editorial and signage work, while the 16 expanded XL styles lean toward display use with greater visual character. Both subfamilies blend geometric construction with humanistic stroke contrast, and support 201 languages with 547 glyphs per style including alternates, swashes and self-building fractions.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Brother XS & XL as an expansion of Brother 1816, adding a condensed version (XS) ideal for narrow spaces without sacrificing legibility, and an expanded version (XL) with strong character for display use. The family mixes geometric shapes with humanistic strokes, allowing designers to select a pure geometric or humanistic feel, or combine XS and XL styles with alternate characters to suit branding, editorial and signage projects in print or on screen.

name: Neftalí
classification_notes: Blends Baroque and Roman serif traditions with calligraphic details, accentuated strokes and wide curves, drawing on Chilean type heritage. The designer cites it as an interpretation of humanist proportions filtered through Baroque and classical Roman models.
summary: A text serif by Franco Jonás drawing on Chilean type traditions and Baroque calligraphy, designed for long-form reading.
description: Designed as an interpretation of Pablo Neruda's 'Poema 20', the family blends Baroque and Roman serif forms with calligraphic details, referencing Chilean typefaces such as Australis and Biblioteca. Twelve styles span four weights with true italics at each, alongside small caps, oldstyle numerals and extensive OpenType features suited to literary and editorial composition. Coverage extends to 203 languages across the Latin script.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Neftalí as a type family designed for continuous reading and editorial design, created as an interpretation of Neruda's 'Poema 20'. It brings together Baroque and Roman styles with features drawn from successful Chilean typefaces, including calligraphic details, medium weights and wide curves intended to echo Neruda's manner of reciting. Winner of the TipoType Award 2015.

name: Mariné Rounded
classification_notes: Described as a geometric sans with humanistic strokes and mild contrast. The rounded terminals and soft stroke endings place it firmly in the geometric sans category with humanist influences.
summary: A geometric sans with rounded terminals and mild humanist contrast, designed to work across both text and display settings.
description: Designed by the TipoType Team, Mariné Rounded softens geometric construction with humanistic stroke qualities, making it usable across a range of sizes and applications. Sixteen styles provide flexibility, and the family includes an Up variant alongside calligraphic OpenType features that add a degree of informality. It supports 145 languages across Latin-based scripts and was named a MyFonts Rising Star in June 2017.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Mariné Rounded as a geometric sans with the softness of humanistic strokes. Its mild contrast and multiple styles allow it to function as both a text and display typeface, and it includes an Up version and calligraphic features for a touch of informality.

name: Mariné
classification_notes: Described explicitly as geometric sans with humanistic stroke qualities; the mild contrast and calligraphic features place it at the intersection of geometric and humanist sans traditions.
summary: A geometric sans with humanist stroke qualities and an informal Up variant, designed for both text and display use across 145 languages.
description: Mariné blends the structure of geometric sans design with the softness of humanist strokes, giving it mild contrast that suits both running text and display settings. The family ships in 16 styles including italic and an Up version with calligraphic features that introduce a lighter, informal register. It is a close cousin of Amelia, with the key differences appearing in the italic and Up cuts.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Mariné as a geometric sans softened by humanistic strokes, with mild contrast that allows it to function across text and display contexts. The family includes an Up version and calligraphic OpenType features for added informality, and covers 145 languages across its 298-character set. It was recognised as a MyFonts Rising Star in May 2014.

name: Rogliano
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Rogliano as a Slab Serif drawing on the industrial alphabets of the Victorian era. Its strong stroke contrast and decorative alternates place it firmly in the display slab tradition.
summary: A 14-style display slab serif rooted in Victorian industrial alphabets, with decorative alternates, borders and ornaments that pull between mechanical directness and humanist warmth.
description: Designed by Rodrigo López Fuentes at Tipotype, Rogliano draws on the strong, direct letterforms of the Industrial Revolution while incorporating stylistic alternates, borders and decorative ornaments from Victorian lithographic posters. The 14-style family spans Thin to Black with italics, covering 199 Latin-based languages and 688 glyphs per style. It moves comfortably between branding and editorial contexts where a historical register is useful without being purely historicist.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Rogliano as an affable Slab Serif that responds to the classic tenet of strong and direct alphabets from the Industrial Revolution, while taking the freedom to flirt with lettering and lithographic posters of the Victorian era through its stylistic alternates, borders and decorative ornaments. They position it as a versatile typeface that can move from mechanical to humanistic with ease, performing efficiently across branding and editorial design, with 14 weights and support for more than 200 Latin languages.

name: Nogal
classification_notes: Described as inspired by early 20th-century sans-serifs with a clean, geometric aesthetic, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans by Rodrigo López Fuentes drawing on early 20th-century sans-serif models, offered in 16 styles across 8 upright and 8 italic cuts.
description: Inspired by early 20th-century geometric sans-serifs, Nogal is designed for versatility across branding, editorial and digital interface work. The family spans five weights from Ultra Light to Black with matching italics, each style carrying over 640 glyphs. Its neutral character makes it serviceable for corporate identities, signage and wayfinding without strong stylistic intrusion.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Nogal as a clean, geometric and modern typeface inspired by early 20th-century sans-serifs, designed by Rodrigo López Fuentes. The complete system offers 16 styles — 8 uprights and 8 italics — each with over 640 glyphs, suited to branding, editorial design, digital interfaces, corporate identities, and signage and wayfinding systems.

name: Rufina Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil variant of the Rufina family, drawing on Bodoni-influenced high-contrast forms with calligraphic touches. The stencil cuts and ornamental alternates place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A stencil adaptation of the Rufina family, pairing Bodoni-influenced high contrast with calligraphic warmth across two weights, their italics and alternate ornamental cuts.
description: Built on the same high-contrast, calligraphic foundation as Rufina, this stencil variant comes in Regular and Bold with matching italics, plus two sets of alternate cuts each including illustrative ornaments. The nine styles cover 147 languages across a 349-character set, with OpenType support for small caps, old-style numerals, ligatures and kerning. Best suited to editorial headlines, packaging and identity work where a refined stencil texture is wanted rather than a utilitarian one.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Rufina Stencil as a family defined by simplicity, delicacy and elegance, born from dark-text on light-background combinations. They characterise it as refined yet distant, blending contrasty Bodoni-influenced forms with the emotive touch of the calligrapher's pen. The family spans two weights with italic counterparts and two sets of alternate cuts, each containing illustrative ornaments.

name: Trasandina
classification_notes: Trasandina blends humanist and geometric models without fully committing to either, placing it in the humanist sans category while acknowledging its geometric tendencies at lighter and heavier weights.
summary: A nine-weight sans-serif by Fernando Díaz that sits between humanist warmth and geometric precision, covering over 200 languages with hinting for screen use.
description: Designed following Luc(as) de Groot's interpolation theory, it spans from Thin to Ultra across nine weights with matching italics, each style carrying 806 characters. The lighter and heavier cuts lean geometric and suit display work, while the middle weights read as neutral and legible at small sizes on screen. It received a Tipos Latinos 2016 Certificate of Excellence and a MyFonts Rising Star designation in August 2015.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe Trasandina as a modern, versatile workhorse with a special personality derived from a mix of humanist and geometric influences, sitting far from both extremes. The family covers 9 weights plus matching italics, with over 800 characters per style, extensive OpenType features, and support for more than 200 languages. It was built with hinting instructions for reliable on-screen performance at all sizes.

name: Environmental Awareness Code
classification_notes: The page contains no typeface information; this is a sustainability policy document, not a typeface page.
summary: No typeface information is present on this page; it is TipoType's environmental sustainability policy published in August 2024.
description: The page at this URL is a corporate sustainability document, not a typeface product page. It outlines TipoType's environmental commitments, including their operation from Uruguay where over 80% of electricity comes from renewable sources. No typeface name, design details, weights, or specimen information are provided.
foundry_blurb: TipoType's environmental awareness code, published August 2024, states the foundry's commitment to sustainable practices including use of renewable energy infrastructure in Uruguay and recyclable materials, with a goal of operating on 100% renewable energy.

name: Tipotype
classification_notes: This is a foundry about page rather than a specific typeface page; no typeface is described, so category defaults cannot be meaningfully assigned. Filed as sans-serif grotesque as a placeholder.
summary: An independent Argentine type foundry operating since 2006, focused on corporate typographic projects for governments, NGOs and private clients across Latin America, Europe and beyond.
description: Founded in 2006, Tipotype has built its practice around producing, adapting and licensing typefaces for institutional and corporate clients across Latin America, the USA, the UK, Germany, the Netherlands and India. The foundry keeps a deliberately lean structure, meaning client contact goes directly to the founding directors. Beyond commercial work, the team has been involved in organising the Letras Latinas and Tipos Latinos biennials, publishing books on type, and teaching at university level.
foundry_blurb: Tipotype describe themselves as an independent type foundry focused on corporate projects, with over fifteen years of work in communication and design globally. They produce by researching market trends, adapt products to specific settings, and advise clients on licensing. The founding team has played an active role in the Latin American typographic industry through awards, biennials, university teaching and large-scale design projects.

foundry_blurb: Tipotype present themselves as an independent type foundry based in Uruguay, active since 2006, specialising in corporate typographic projects. They describe producing, adapting and licensing typefaces for clients across Uruguay, Latin America, the United States, the United Kingdom, Germany, the Netherlands and India, and highlight their role in organising the Letras Latinas and Tipos Latinos Latin American type biennials.
